NameTranslationKey.Description: Translation key for NPC name display

Instructions.$Comment: Wave at player when they approach
Watching state - player is nearby, just watch them

Adventurer-specific: notice nearby hostile creatures, mirroring the real Trork combat AI's own threat sensor (Type: Mob, Prioritiser: Attitude/Hostile) - feeds npc-ai-stack's ThreatMemory so the AI can react to it in conversation. LockOnTarget makes the found hostile the locked "Target" the chase/attack nodes below react to.

Reactive companion defense: real, shipped 'EntityEvent' sensor (EventType: Damage, SearchType: PlayerOnly, NPCGroup: Player - confirmed valid asset at Server/NPC/Groups/Player.json) - confirmed via disassembly of SensorEntityEvent/BuilderSensorEntityEvent AND via its real usage in Server/NPC/Roles/_Core/Components/Steps/Component_Instruction_Allied_Damage_Check.json. Fires the instant a nearby player (the one this NPC is following) takes damage, regardless of whether THIS companion currently has line of sight on whatever's hitting them - the Mob+Attitude sensor above only reacts to a hostile the companion can already see/sense itself, which could miss an attacker sneaking up on the player specifically. Combined here with the same Mob+Attitude hostile search, just widened to 45 blocks (vs. the normal 30) - we now know for certain a threat is imminent nearby, so it's worth searching harder for it. Not yet live-confirmed (this whole companion-combat feature has never been tested near a real hostile creature - see hytale-plugin/README.md's "Known simplification").

Real, shipped 'Damage' sensor Type (Combat: true) - confirmed via disassembly of SensorDamage/BuilderSensorDamage AND via its real usage in Server/NPC/Roles/_Core/Components/Steps/Component_Instruction_Damage_Check.json (the neutral Kweebec's own Panic trigger). Reports a player physically attacking this NPC as a real outcome (personality.py's player_attacked_npc) - see NoteAttackedByPlayerAction.java. Not yet live-confirmed against a real attack (this NPC role sets Invulnerable:true; grounded in PlayerDamageFilterSystem's own pattern of cancelling damage via a flag on an event that still fires, rather than suppressing the event entirely, but that's inference from disassembly, not a live test).
Return to Idle when player leaves (clear animation first)

Companion combat, melee range: once tamed, if a hostile is locked as Target and close enough, attack it. Real "Attack" action (confirmed via disassembly of BuilderActionAttack, and via real shipped usage in Component_Instruction_Attack_Sequence_Spirit.json/Component_Instruction_Intelligent_Idle_Goblin.json) - "Root_NPC_Attack_Melee" is the same generic melee attack pattern the real, shipped Tamed_Cow.json uses, not a made-up interaction id. This node has no Continue, so it takes priority over chasing/following below whenever it matches.
Companion combat, chase: hostile locked as Target but not yet in melee range - close the distance. Takes priority over following the player (no Continue) whenever a hostile is being chased.

Guiding: the AI decided OFFER_GUIDE (see llm_client.py) and GuideState.startGuiding() was called - walk toward NearbyLandmarks.closestPosition() until SeekLandmarkSensor reports arrival (it stops matching automatically, no separate arrival check needed here). Takes priority over merely following the player (no Continue), but combat above still wins if a hostile is being fought/chased.

Companion mode: once tamed, seek toward the nearest player instead of standing still (no hostile currently being fought/chased, and not actively guiding - see the nodes above, which take priority). The Seek/MaintainDistance BodyMotion types are real, shipped engine functionality (confirmed via disassembly and via the real Template_Livestock.json's own tamed-animal follow behavior), not custom pathfinding code. v1 simplification: follows the nearest player, not specifically its owner - correct for solo/small-group testing, would need real owner-specific entity filtering for a populated multiplayer world (see CompanionState.java's javadoc)
Interaction state - look at the talking player

Keep following even while starting a conversation - $Interaction only lasts ~1s before the Timeout below returns to Watching, but without this an established companion visibly freezes for that second every time the player clicks to talk. Continue:true is required here so the Timeout node below still runs every tick regardless of whether this one matches - without it, a nearby companion would get stuck in $Interaction forever.
Return to Watching after interaction ends
